revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} held at [[Shinto shrine|a shrine]] every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingu calendar|lt=Jingu calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
== Reisai at various Shrines == {| class="wikitable" !shrine ! Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|a shrine]] every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingu calendar|lt=Jingu calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
== Reisai at various Shrines == {| class="wikitable" !shrine ! Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingu calendar|lt=Jingu calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shinto ritual|WD=Q109937652}} held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|a shrine]] every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=aki matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shinto ritual|WD=Q109937652}} held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|a shrine]] every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref> [[sn:例祭]] The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=aki matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingu calendar|lt=Jingu calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
== Reisai at various Shrines == {| class="wikitable" !shrine ! Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
== Reisai at various Shrines == {| class="wikitable" !shrine ! Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Festival Reisai]][[ja:例祭]][[simple:Reisai]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Festival Reisai]][[ja:例祭]][[simple:Reisai]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Festival Reisai]][[ja:例祭]][[simple:Reisai]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shinto ritual|WD=Q109937652}} held at [[:en:Shinto shrine|a shrine]] every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />
== History == The name "Reisai" began to be used in [[:en:Early modern period|the early modern period]] ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />
Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />
After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
== Reisai at various Shrines == {| class="wikitable" !shrine ! Regular festival day |- | [[:en:Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Shrine]] | [[:en:February 11|February 11th]] |- | [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] | [[:en:March 13|March 13th]] |- | [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] | [[:en:April 9|April 9]] |- | [[:en:Katori Shrine|Katori Shrine]] | [[:en:April 14|April 14th]] |- | [[:en:Heian Shrine|Heian Shrine]] | [[:en:April 15|April 15]] |- | [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Ōkunitama Shrine]] | [[:en:May 5|May 5]] |- | [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]] | [[:en:May 14|May 14]] |- | [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] | [[:en:May 15|May 15]] |- | [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]] | [[:en:June 5|June 5]] |- | [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] | [[:en:June 15|June 15]] |- | [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine]] | [[:en:August 1|Aug. 1]] |- | [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] | [[:en:September 1|September 1st]] |- | [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]] | [[:en:September 15|September 15]] |- | [[:en:Dazaifu Tenmangū|Dazaifu Tenmangū]] | [[:en:September 25|September 25]] |- | [[:en:Mononobe Shrine|Mononobe Shrine]] | [[:en:October 9|October 9]] |- | [[:en:Isonokami Shrine|Isonokami Shrine]] | [[:en:October 15|October 15]] |- | [[:en:Meiji Shrine|Meiji Shrine]] | [[:en:November 3|November 3]] |}

Following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />

At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />

== History ==
The name "Reisai" began to be used in {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|WD=Q5308718|lt=the early modern period}} ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />

At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />

After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shinto ritual|WD=Q109937652}} held at {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=a shrine}} every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />

== History ==
The name "Reisai" began to be used in {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|WD=Q5308718|lt=the early modern period}} ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />

After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=heihaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />

After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Festival Reisai]][[ja:例祭]][[simple:Reisai]]


A '''reisai''' is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀 (神道)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Shinto ritual|WD=Q109937652}} held at {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=a shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} every year and is considered the most important.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2023-12-07|title=Reisai {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231207210606/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9282|access-date=2024-04-07|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

The annual festival, or Reisai, is held once a year, often on a day of special historical significance to the {{ill|10=ja|11=deity|Saijin|lt=enshrined deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or the shrine itself. For shrines dedicated to deities who were historical figures, this festival often coincides with the figure's birthday or death anniversary. In cases where there is no specific historical date, the festival is typically held during the {{ill|10=ja|11=春祭り|lt=spring|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} or {{ill|10=ja|11=秋祭り|lt=autumn|autumn festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} as a general celebration. The date of the Reisai is fixed and cannot be arbitrarily changed. Following {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, shrines under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] are required to obtain approval from the Association if there is a need to change the date of the Reisai.<ref name=":0" />

== History ==
The name "Reisai" began to be used in {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|WD=Q5308718|lt=the early modern period|1=Early modern period}} ; before that, the festival was called {{ill|10=ja|11=大祭|Omatsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Grand Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438341}} (Grand Festival) or Onmatsuri (Imperial Festival), or the shrine's name was added to the word "matsuri" (festival), resulting in names such as Kasuga Festival or Iwashimizu Festival. Even today, this name is recorded in the "Shoreki" section of the {{ill|Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|lt=Jingū calendar|ja|神宮暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū calendar|qq=}}. In the early modern period, illustrated {{ill|Meisho zue|fr|lt=guides to famous places|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meisho zue|qq=}} around the country would often include entries such as "XX Shrine Annual Festival on XX Month XX Day," which shows that it had become common to refer to important festivals that represent a shrine as annual festivals.<ref name=":0" />

Under the Meiji shrine system, annual festivals, along with [[Kinensai|the Niiname-sai]] and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Kinen-sai festivals]], were designated as 'great festivals', and [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] offering {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|sacred offerings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=Hanpei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11481885}} were sent to national and prefectural shrines, local shrines, village shrines, and some village shrines (shrines designated for the offering of sacred offerings) to make offerings from the state and the imperial family.<ref name=":0" />

After the Second World War, the system of offerings of sacred offerings from the government was abolished, but [[Chokusaisha|imperial envoys]] still visit imperial shrines during annual festivals. At other shrines, instead of the official system of offering heihaku, the Association of Shinto Shrines offers 'Honchohei' (offerings from the Association of Shinto Shrines) (however, this is limited to shrines that are under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] ).
